w03.title: Energy and Water Efficiency of Dishwashers
w03.topic: 3.2 Energy and water efficiency of dishwashers

tech.body: Students can explain how dishwashers consume energy (heating water) and water per cycle.

math.body: Students calculate annual costs using realistic usage scenarios.

env.body: Students compare energy and water consumption across dishwasher programmes.

green.body: Students read energy labels, choose appliance settings, compare annual costs of different programmes and evaluate environmental impact.

w03.resources: Calculator, electricity tariff (e.g., 0.30 €/kWh), water tariff (e.g., 0.002 €/l), EU energy label for a dishwasher (from home or manufacturer website).
w03.intro: A modern dishwasher can actually use less water than washing by hand — but only if it is loaded correctly and run on the right programme. The choice between an intensive 70 °C programme and an eco programme can make a significant difference to your electricity bill and water use. In this worksheet you will investigate those differences and decide when each programme makes sense.

theory.title: Theory
theory.intro: A dishwasher uses most of its energy to heat water. It also uses a small amount for the drying cycle (heating element).
theory.formulaNote: Simplified: ~0.05 kWh per litre per 10 °C temperature rise.

intensive.label: Intensive programme (70 °C):
intensive.body: Uses more water (18–20 l) and more electricity (1.5–2.0 kWh) — meant for pots and heavily soiled dishes.

auto.label: Auto programme:
auto.body: Sensors adjust temperature and duration — convenient but often less efficient than Eco.

eco.label: Eco programme (50 °C):
eco.body: Long cycle (3–4 hours) but lower temperature. Typical: 0.8–1.0 kWh, 10–12 l of water.

hand.label: Hand-washing comparison:
hand.body: A running tap uses 6–12 l of water per minute. Washing dishes for 10 minutes with the tap half-open = 60–120 l — far more than a dishwasher.

label.label: Eco label:
label.body: Since 2021, dishwashers carry an EU energy label rating from A (best) to G. The label shows: energy per cycle (kWh), water per cycle (l), noise level (dB), and programme duration.

half.label: Half-load:
half.body: Running a half-loaded machine wastes energy and water. A half-load setting typically saves 20–30 % on both. Otherwise, waiting for a full load is more efficient.

theory.videos: Suggested videos for an intro to how dishwashers work and the difference between quick and eco cycles:
theory.video1: How Does a Dishwasher Actually Work?

example.title: Worked example
example.intro: A household runs the dishwasher 250 times per year.

act1.q1: How many times does your household run the dishwasher per year? ____ cycles/year.
act1.q2: Calculate the annual cost for each programme at your usage frequency.
act1.q3: How much money is saved per year by using Eco instead of Intensive?
act1.q4: How much water is saved per year (in litres) at your usage frequency?
act1.q5: Why does the Eco programme take over 3 hours? Hint: think about heating rate and insulation.

act2.intro: A person hand-washes dishes for 12 minutes with the tap at 8 l/min. The water is heated by an electric boiler (0.05 kWh/l to heat from 10 °C to 40 °C).
act2.q1: Calculate the total water used for hand-washing.
act2.q2: Calculate the energy required to heat that water (kWh).
act2.q3: Calculate the total cost for hand-washing (water + electricity).
act2.q4: Compare to the Eco programme cost from Activity 1. Which is cheaper?
act2.q5: For what types of washing loads is the intensive programme justified? Give two real examples.
act2.q6: If the school canteen runs 10 dishwasher cycles per school day (160 days/year) on the intensive programme, calculate the annual energy cost. Then recalculate if they switch to Eco. What is the annual saving?
act2.q7: Find an EU energy label for a dishwasher. What is the difference in annual energy (kWh/year) between A and E rating?

act3.q1: Calculate the CO₂ emissions from 365 intensive cycles (use 0.27 kg CO₂/kWh).
act3.q2: How does switching to Eco reduce the annual carbon footprint in kg CO₂?
act3.q3: Water cost is 0.003 €/l. Recalculate the annual water saving in euros using this tariff. Does it change the economic argument for Eco?

peer.title: Peer-to-peer tip
peer.dykBody: Scrape, don’t rinse! Modern dishwashers don’t need pre-rinsing.
peer.q1: How does pre-rinsing waste water and energy?

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.prompt: When would hand-washing be more efficient than a dishwasher? Explain in your own words and refer to your calculations from Activity 2.

ee.title: Additional tasks for electrical engineering students

ee1.intro: Draw a block diagram showing: control board, heating element, circulation pump, drain pump, NTC thermistor, door interlock switch, detergent dispenser and flood protection sensor. Indicate typical power ratings.

ee2.intro: Modern dishwashers use BLDC (brushless DC) motors for the circulation pump. List three advantages of BLDC over universal motors. Why are they more expensive?

ee3.intro: How is the water temperature regulated in a dishwasher? Explain the control loop.

ee4.intro: List three safety devices in a dishwasher and briefly explain the role of each.

ee5.intro: An NTC thermistor has resistance 10 kΩ at 25 °C and 2 kΩ at 60 °C. It is used in a voltage divider with a 5 V supply and a 10 kΩ fixed resistor. Calculate the output voltage at 25 °C and at 60 °C. How does the control board use this signal?
